coredata.dc:title: Green fabrication of graphene quantum dots from cotton with CaSiO3 nanostructure and enhanced photocatalytic performance for water treatment
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Karami, Maryam
Yousif, Qahtan A.
Ghanbari, Mojgan
Mahdavi, Kamran
Salavati-Niasari, Masoud
coredata.dc:description: Perovskite nanostructures have become one of the most alluring classes of materials because of their structure and properties, which have revolutionized many areas of electronics, optoelectronics, and photonics. In this research, CaSiO3 nanostructures have been prepared by hydrothermal synthesis as profitable and light-active materials for photocatalytic activities. The photocatalytic procedure is an eco-friendly method, which is recognized as an effective alternative for the degradation of multiple hazardous materials. In this regard, photocatalytic properties of oxide perovskite have been developed by effective incorporation of CaSiO3 nanostructures with graphene quantum dots (GQDs) under facile green low-cost hydrothermal conditions. The impact of two factors, including diverse varieties of surfactants and concentration of GQDs, was surveyed through XRD, FESEM images, and diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS) on purity, structure, and photocatalytic performance. The results demonstrated that the CaSiO3/GQDs in high-level concentration showed higher visible-light photocatalytic activity. Besides, the kinetics of photocatalytic reactions was investigated, and the outcomes showed that higher photocatalytic performance possessed a higher reaction rate coefficient.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Oxide perovskite
Graphene quantum dots
Photocatalytic activities
Visible-light

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD pattern of samples (a) 1, (b) 2 (c) 3, (d) 4, (e) 5, (f) 6, (g) 7, and (h) GQDs.
SEM images of the samples (a) 1, (b) 2, (c) 3, (d) 4, (e) 5, (f) 6, (g) 7 and (h) size distribution diagram of the samples.
EDS spectrum of the samples (a) 1, (b) 2, (c) 3, (d) 4, (e) 5, (f) 6, and (g) 7.
DRS spectrum and optical density (αhν)2 vs. energy (E) plot of the samples (a and b) 5 and (c and d) 7.
(a) Low temperature N2 adsorption/desorption isotherm, (b) BET plot, (c) BJH plot of sample 6, (d) Low temperature N2 adsorption/desorption isotherm, (e) BET plot, and (f) BJH plot of sample 7.
Photocatalytic destruction and plots of ln(C0/C) vs time in the presence of six different dyes: (a and b) sample 5, (c and d) sample 6, and (e and f) sample 7.
Photocatalytic destruction and plots of ln(C0/C) vs time of specimen 7 over different concentrations of AR: (a and b) 10 ppm, (c and d) 15 ppm, and (e and f) 20 ppm.
(a and b) The effect of pH, (c) decolorization of AR in the presence of BQ, BA, and EDTA as scavenger, and (d) cycling runs in the photocatalytic degradation of AR for sample 7.
Schematic diagram of the fabrication of CaSiO3, GQDs, and CaSiO3/GQDs nanocomposites.
Schematic diagram of photocatalytic mechanism of CaSiO3/GQDs nanocomposites.
Preparation conditions for CaSiO3 and CaSiO3/GQDs nanocomposites.
The surface characteristics related to CaSiO3/GQDs nanocomposites.
The photocatalytic activity of GQDs doped with different material.

head.ce:title: Green fabrication of graphene quantum dots from cotton with CaSiO3 nanostructure and enhanced photocatalytic performance for water treatment

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Nano Science and Nano Technology, University of Kashan, Kashan, P. O. Box. 87317-51167, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Nano Science and Nano Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Nano Science and Nano Technology, University of Kashan, Kashan, P. O. Box. 87317-51167, I. R. Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Education, University of Al-Qadisiyah, Al Diwaniyah, Iraq
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, College of Education, University of Al-Qadisiyah, Al Diwaniyah, Iraq

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Perovskite nanostructures have become one of the most alluring classes of materials because of their structure and properties, which have revolutionized many areas of electronics, optoelectronics, and photonics. In this research, CaSiO3 nanostructures have been prepared by hydrothermal synthesis as profitable and light-active materials for photocatalytic activities. The photocatalytic procedure is an eco-friendly method, which is recognized as an effective alternative for the degradation of multiple hazardous materials. In this regard, photocatalytic properties of oxide perovskite have been developed by effective incorporation of CaSiO3 nanostructures with graphene quantum dots (GQDs) under facile green low-cost hydrothermal conditions. The impact of two factors, including diverse varieties of surfactants and concentration of GQDs, was surveyed through XRD, FESEM images, and diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS) on purity, structure, and photocatalytic performance. The results demonstrated that the CaSiO3/GQDs in high-level concentration showed higher visible-light photocatalytic activity. Besides, the kinetics of photocatalytic reactions was investigated, and the outcomes showed that higher photocatalytic performance possessed a higher reaction rate coefficient.

ce:section.ce:para: Today, accelerated increment in the global population and industrial development has led to massive ecological contamination produced by various organic pollutants and toxic wastes. The trend of discharging contamination, including organic colorants in water supplies, causes water infection and not only causing potential harm to people's health but also the lives of aquatic organisms and many other ecosystems [1]. Based on the Lancet Commission on Pollution and Health, approximately 1.8 million fatalities in the world are related to diseases transmitted from contaminated water [2]. Many different substances such as inorganic compounds (salts, metal complexes, metal oxides), organic compounds (pesticides, dyes, fertilizers and pharmaceutical ingredients), agricultural wastewater, pesticides, and pathogens have become a major source of environmental and water contamination [3]. According to the current condition, the development of advanced technologies for water purification with cost-effective and high performance is favorable. Most prevalent water purification processes such as precipitation, filtration, chemical, and membrane methods are feasible for water treatment, but as a drawback, they only transfer pollutants from one environment to another, and in addition to the high costs required to operate them, they can also cause secondary toxic pollution in the ecosystem [4]. The most attractive feature for water treatment is Advanced Oxidation Processes (AOPs), which include various processes such as Fenton, photo-Fenton, ozonolysis, photocatalysis, and photolysis. All these processes generate oxygen-derived radicals and especially hydroxyl radicals. The best aspect of this method is that creating strong radicals with high potential allows the radical as an oxidizer to mineralize a wide range of organic chemical materials without selectivity and completely convert the organic compounds into carbon dioxide and mineral acid [5–7]. Among all the methods of AOPs, photocatalysis has been extensively investigated for the purification of wastewater. Photocatalysis is usually recognized as the chemical reaction caused by the absorption of photons by solids that are photocatalysts [8]. When photocatalyst is exposed to light, the electrons (e–) and holes (h+) are formed, and they lead to the reduction/oxidation process on the surface of photocatalytst with their equal or more energy than the band gap of photocatalyst [9,10]. In addition, structure, surface area, and composite materials are unavoidable factors in materials science, and they perform a significant character in the efficiency of the catalyst in photocatalytic activities [11,12]. Heterogeneous photocatalysts have allured significant concentration in current decades due to their widespread use and better efficiency [13]. Among all the strategies for the synthesis of heterogeneous photocatalysts, quantum dots (QDs) have been studied as a suitable alternative for covering the UV spectrum as well as visible sunlight [14]. Besides all studied advantages for the photocatalytic ability of these materials, they are not progressed substantially because the rate of recombination of excited e– and h+ pairs develops due to the efficient surface traps of QDs. In addition, QDs become extremely unstable to hole oxidation when they are exposed to light in a liquid medium [15]. Therefore, researchers are still trying to produce materials heterojunctions under stable conditions and explore ways to improve their properties and fabricate eco-friendly, high-performance, recyclable sensitizers to enhance optical activity [16]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are the new generation of QD materials with various potential applications in solar cells, sensors, light emitting devices, photocatalysis and having a large number of physicochemical properties. Although conventional semiconductor QDs are toxic and unstable, GQDs are highly resistant to severe chemical attacks or ultraviolet radiation in addition to being environmentally friendly [17,18]. GQDs have a large exciton binding energy (∼0.8 eV determined for 2 nm GQDs) which causes a significant recombination rate for photoexcited e– and h+. Therefore, GQDs in the pure state shows the weak catalytic performance [19]. Research has shown that doping is a highly effective technique for altering the electron density of substances and improving their transport and optical characteristics [20]. Herein, we report a simple, low-cost one-step route to prepare GQDs from cotton cellulose. The synthetic mechanism is inferred that the formation of GQDs is composed of two steps, i. e., the hydrolyzation of cellulose to generate glucose at first, and the follow-up cyclic condensation of glucose to obtain GQDs. The whole process can be detailed as follows: Firstly, the deionized water produced a large amount of H+ and OH at high temperature and pressure under hydrothermal condition. Thus, the hydrolysis of cellulose took place in succession under the action of H+, in the absence of any acids or cellulose. Afterwards, a variety of components, such as glucose, cellobiose, and levoglucosan were generated from cellulose. With the reaction of hydrolysis progressing, the product yield of glucose became higher and higher, and the glucose turned into the main product. Consequently, the hydroxyl groups of glucose reacted with the hydrogen atoms of an adjacent one, while the formyl groups reacted with the hydroxyl groups, leading to the dehydration of glucose under the hydrothermal condition. Simultaneously, the carbon atoms of the glucoses covalently links with one another to generate the aromatic rings, the elementary unit of the graphene structure. Thus, the glucoses were converted into the GQDs through the cyclic condensation. At the same time, the carbide precipitates were also generated from the residues of the carbohydrates by the carbonization. In the end, the GQDs solution was obtained through the centrifugalization via separating the precipitates out from the reaction products [21].

In this work, we evaluate the photocatalytic properties and photodegradation of organic dyes in water for oxide perovskite nanostructures of CaSiO3 in the pure state and doping with two different concentrations of GQDs. Calcium silicate (CaSiO3) ceramic as a perovskite compound has many compatibilities, such as special mechanical strength (294 MPa) [22], having low sintering temperature (<1350 °C), which is even lower than alumina and silicon carbide, and owing to the abundance of calcium and silicon elements in the earth's crust, they can be used as low-cost materials [23]. Moreover, CaSiO3 perovskites have acceptable biodegradability, stability against heat, and low dielectric loss. Therefore, CaSiO3 can be used as a capable option to construct ceramics, which can be broadly considered in bone tissue engineering, heat resistant compounds, and high-frequency insulators [24,25]. Research has shown the CaSiO3 perovskites are prepared by different methods. For example, preparation of CaSiO3 ceramics with less compact structure by the sol-gel route, with sintering samples at 1320 °C [26], and also using the hydrothermal method to construct CaSiO3 nanowires as a promising structure for photoluminescent functions [27]. Also, CaSiO3 (wollastonite) is a mineral that can be used in the fabrication of special radio ceramics, hygienic components, porcelain materials, lining bricks, glaze, and flux [28]. One-dimensional (1D) CaSiO3 nanostructures are used in many applications such as polymer fillers and used as bioactive materials. Also, they have been prepared with various methods, especially the NaCl/NP-9 molten-salt synthesis (MSS) or organic surfactant/solvent/microwave-assisted hydrothermal-thermal conversion (HTC) [27]. In this paper, both nanostructures of CaSiO3 and GQDs have been synthesized by the hydrothermal method. Hydrothermal is a successful method to prepare several crystals such as quartz and malachite. Moreover, it can be used to grow single crystals without displacement, and crystals in this process have a better crystallinity than other methods [29,30]. The effect of different types of surfactants was studied on the structure and purity of products. Finally, the samples were examined via XRD, SEM, EDX, UV-Vis, BET, FT-IR, and TEM to gain the desired product. The photocatalytic properties of pure CaSiO3 and CaSiO3/GQDs nanocomposites were studied to compare their activities for organic dye degradation in the water. These outcomes designate that CaSiO3/GQDs nanocomposites can work as an efficient agent for higher levels of degradation under visible light.

All raw materials in this research were utilized within the analytic standard. Sodium metasilicate (Na2SiO3), Calcium chloride (CaCl2), Sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS), Polyvinyl pyrrolidone (PVP-25000), Polyethylene glycol (PEG-6000), Ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA), 1,4-Benzoquinone (BQ), benzoic acid (BA), and Sodium salicylate (NaHSal) were obtained from Merck Company and used without further purification.

In a typical synthesis, certain amounts of cotton were first washed with water and dried in the oven, and then transferred into a beaker with 25 mL DI water. Then, the suspension was moved into a stainless steel autoclave and placed into a muffle furnace at 200 °C for 12 h. The final product was soluble in water. After chilling down to ambient temperature, the brown solution was filtered and centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 15 min to remove larger particles (Scheme 1 ).

CaSiO3 nanoparticles were synthesized by the hydrothermal method. Briefly, 0.225 g calcium chloride (CaCl2) was liquefied in 60 ml distilled water. Then, 0.25 g sodium silicate (Na2SiO3) was liquefied in 20 ml distilled water and slowly combined with the calcium chloride solution [27]. After 10 min stirring at 25 °C, the white suspension was transported to a Teflon-lined stainless reactor and heated at 200 °C for 12 h. The white precipitate was washed with deionized water multiple times and dried at a suitable temperature for 18 h. Finally, the white powder was calcined in the muffle furnace at 800 °C for 3 h (Scheme 1). Various kinds of surface-active agents were added to attain the best condition (Table 1 ).

Typically, the hydrothermal method was used to prepare CaSiO3/GQDs nanocomposite. At the first step, 0.2 g of CaSiO3 was combined with 10 ml low concentrated GQDs solution (fabricated in part 2.2) and stirred for 1 h. The suspension was transported to a Teflon-lined stainless reactor and heated at 200 °C for 6 h. Finally, the precipitate was washed and dried in the oven to prepare a low-level doping nanocomposite. The same method was used to fabricate high-level doping nanocomposite with a higher concentration of GQDs solution.

The photocatalytic decomposition was studied for six different dyes, including Acid Red (AR), Acid Yellow (AY), Thymol Blue (TB), Methyl Orange (MO), Eriochrome Black T (EBT), and Rhodamine B (RhB). The catalytic performance of nano-CaSiO3 and its nanocomposite with GQDs was carried out by their performance for the decolorization of toxic colorants below visible radiation (Osram 150 W), having wavelengths between 400 and 780 nm in a beaker containing 50 mL of 10, 15, and 20 ppm of dye solution. For each test, different amounts of photocatalyst (pure CaSiO3 nanostructures or CaSiO3/GQDs nanocomposite) were added to the prepared dye solution. The mix was stirred in the dark for 30 min before the irradiation of visible light. Finally, a 5 ml sample from the mixture was extracted every 15 min and then centrifuged (14,000 rpm for 4 min) to remove the catalysts and were examined by noting the absorption changes.

The X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) method is a valuable way to study the crystalline structure and purity of products. Fig. 1 a shows the XRD patterns of sample 1 (pure CaSiO3). This pattern is in acceptable conformity with the pure phase of the monoclinic structure of CaSiO3 with the JCPDS card No. 075-1396. Fig. 1(b–e) illustrates the XRD patterns of CaSiO3 in the presence of four surfactants to gain the best purity and structure. CaSiO3 with the JCPDS card No. 075-1396 and a specific amount of unknown phase were formed in the presence of SDS (Fig. 1b) as an anionic surface-active agent, PVP-25000 (Fig. 1c) and PEG-6000 (Fig. 1d) as a polymeric capping agent. As shown in Fig. 1e, CaSiO3 was formed with the reference code of 075–1396 in sample 5. The diffraction peaks have more crystallinity with the addition of NaHSal as a complexing capping agent. Hence, NaHSal was elected as the most desirable surfactant for additional operations. Fig. 1f and g present the XRD patterns of two different nanocomposites of CaSiO3 with diluted GQDs solution and concentrated GQDs solution, respectively, and in both samples (6-7) CaSiO3 was formed with the JCPDS card No. 075-1396. As depicted in Fig. 1, due to the limited quantity and large dispersion of GQDs in the composite, no characteristic diffraction peak can be detected in the nanocomposites of CaSiO3/GQDs (sample 6-7) and the XRD pattern of CaSiO3 in the pure phase (sample 5). Fig. 1h shows the XRD pattern of GQDs, which clearly illustrates the amorphous peak (approximately at 2θ = 24°) and demonstrates the amorphous structure of GQDs [31,32]. Finally, the crystallite size of all samples was determined by the Scherrer equation (D = Kλ/βcosθ) [33]. As shown in Table 1, the crystallite sizes of all samples are in the range of 14–36 nm.

The photoluminescence (PL) spectrum of GQDs has been used due to their high potential for characterization of charge transfer, migration, and recombination processes in photocatalysts [34]. Less possibility of recombination of electron-hole pairs in substance can be demonstrated with lower intensity in the photoluminescence spectra [35]. The optical properties of the as-synthesized GQDs solution were measured by photoluminescence (PL) spectra, which are illustrated in Fig. 2 . Although the specific mechanism of PL spectra for GQDs is not accessible yet, it can be announced that it may happen due to the e–-h+ recombination, quantum effect, edge shape, free zig-zag sites with a carbene-like triplet ground state, or defections in surface functional groups of the GQDs [36]. As shown in Fig. 2, the excitation wavelength was changed from almost 320 to 380 nm. In addition, the figure illustrates that the position of emission peak shifts from 450 nm to longer wavelengths and with lower intensity at 530 nm, which indicates an effective transfer of the photoexcited electrons. Also, further experimental results of photocatalytic tests confirm that the GQDs increase the photocatalytic capability of CaSiO3 via doping.

Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy is a widely used analysis in the study of components, surface functional groups, and the structure of products with selecting vibrations relating to single chemical groups involved in a particular reaction [37]. As seen in Fig. 3 , the FT-IR spectrum depicts the characteristic absorption bands of GQDs. The result indicates different absorption bands at 3427, 2928, 1720, 1614, 1383, and 1112 cm−1, which are related to the stretching vibrations of O–H, C–H, C=O, C=C, C–N and C–O, respectively [38,39]. These results demonstrate that stretching vibrations correspond to carbonyl, carboxyl, hydroxyl, epoxy groups on the surface of GQDs in the reaction [40].

The field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) micrographs of all samples are illustrated in Fig. 4 to study the influence of addition various surface-active agents and different levels of doping on the morphology of samples. As can be seen, without surfactants, nanoparticles in sample 1 (Fig. 4a) are formed approximately similar to the samples with surfactants (Fig. 4b-e). Also, nanostructures are almost identical in particle size. Therefore, four surfactants, such as SDS, PVP, PEG-6000, and NaHsal, revealed the same effect on samples 2-5, respectively (Fig. 4(b–e)). According to Fig. 4e, the particles in the presence of NaHsal were formed in a better structure and shape, and the degree of aggregation of particles is less than other samples. Therefore, sample 5 was selected as the optimal condition for scrutinizing the influence of surfactants on morphology for subsequent reactions. The FESEM images of two nanocomposites of CaSiO3/GQDs (Sample 6 and 7) are depicted in Fig. 4(f and g). As illustrated in the images, adding GQDs solution to create doped products causes agglomeration in the structure and morphology of the nanocomposites. According to Fig. 4g, due to the high concentration of GQDs solution and high level of doping, the degree of aggregation and irregularity is more severe than sample 6 (Fig. 4), with the low level of doping and dilute solution of GQDs. The particle diameters of sample 5 were estimated by the Digimizer software, to be between 50 and 80 nm. The average diameters of the samples are listed in Table 1, and the size distribution diagram of all samples is depicted in Fig. 4h. Fig. 5 (a and b) illustrates the TEM photographs of sample 5 (pure CaSiO3) in two different scales (60 and 20 nm). The average size of the nanoparticles was estimated at 60 nm, which is in acceptable accordance with the results of XRD and FESEM.

Fig. 6 (a–g) depicts the EDX spectra of CaSiO3 and CaSiO3/GQDs nanocomposites, respectively. The spectra of CaSiO3 in different conditions show that the samples comprise Ca, Si, and O elements. In addition to the mentioned elements, the spectra of CaSiO3/GQDs confirm the presence of carbon (C) in the samples. Also, given the A% and W% of each element, it is clear that all products are of high purity and close to the XRD results.

Fig. 7 (a and c) shows the UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS) spectra of samples 5 and 7, respectively. The DRS spectroscopy was used to compare and obtain further information on the optical absorption characteristics of two different samples. This figure unveils a typical absorption from 200 to 450 nm. According to Fig. 7, the absorption bands at 351 nm are associated with the maximum adsorption in the bandgap of this material, and some discrete absorption bands can be designated to vacancies or impurities in the structure. The optical bandgap (Eg) was estimated according to the optical absorption spectrum employing the following formula: (Ahυ)n = B(hυ-Eg) [41,42]. In this equation, n is 1/2 for indirect and 2 for direct transitions; A is absorbent; hυ is the photon energy; and B is a material constant. The linear segment of the (Ahυ)n curve vs. hυ to zero illustrates the optical bandgap to the absorption band via extrapolating. As shown in Fig. 7 (b and d), the bandgaps of samples 5 (CaSiO3) and 7 (CaSiO3/GQDs high-level doping) were determined 3.4 eV for sample 5 (Figs. 7b) and 2.55 eV for sample 7 (Fig. 7d). Although the calculated bandgap of sample 7 has decreased compared to sample 5 (pure CaSiO3), CaSiO3/GQDs nanocomposite can be an effective nanocatalyst due to its predicted bandgap. The difference in bandgap values of pure CaSiO3 and CaSiO3/GQDs is related to the crystal structure of materials.

BET analysis was applied to study the physical adsorption, and Table 2 indicates the average pore size, total pore volume, and specific surface areas of sample 6 (CaSiO3/GQDs low-level doping) and 7 (CaSiO3/GQDs high-level doping). Low-temperature N2 adsorption/desorption diagrams, BET plots, and BJH plots of as-fabricated nanocomposites are illustrated in Fig. 8 , which shows the nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherm and plots from sample 6 (Fig. 8a–c) and sample 7 (Fig. 8d–f). Conforming to IUPAC categorization, isotherms are defined in type III, and H3-type hysteresis is assigned to microporous or mesoporous substances arranged for specimens 6 and 7. As shown in Fig. 8 (b and e), samples own a widespread pore size dispersion from mesopore to micropore due to the specific comparative pressure. According to Table 2, the specific BET surface area of samples 6 and 7 was determined 65.44 and 16.16 m2g−1, respectively, and the average pore diameter was 10.14 and 22.59 nm for samples 6 and 7. Additionally, the pore volume of samples 6 and 7 estimated from the BJH model was 0.166 and 0.091 cm3 g−1, respectively. Finally, the BET information is in an acceptable match with the results of TEM and FESEM photographs.

The photocatalytic ability of pure CaSiO3 nanostructures and CaSiO3/GQDs nanocomposites was studied by scrutinizing the photodegradation of six different toxic dyes as pollutants, such as Acid Red (AR), Acid Yellow (AY), Methyl Orange (MO), Thymol Blue (TB), Eriochrome Black T (EBT), and Rhodamine B (RhB) in water-soluble phase and exposed to visible light (Fig. 9 ). In the absence of light or the nanostructures, almost no dyes were destroyed after 120 min, which means the self-degradation portion was insignificant. The rate of decolorization (%D) was defined as follows: %D = (C0-Ct)/C0 × 100.

In this equation, C0 and Ct are the absorbance of the liquid sample before and after degradation, severally [43]. The impression of various colorants on three distinct samples was surveyed to choose higher photocatalytic efficiency. The photocatalytic performance of pure CaSiO3 (sample 5) was investigated in the presence of AR, and destruction percentage was 38.5% after 120 min (Fig. 9a). Fig. 9c shows the photocatalytic degradation of sample 6 in the presence of AR, AY, MO, TB, EBT, and RhB. The destruction of dye for CaSiO3/GQDs nanocomposites (sample 6) over these dyes was about 65.7%, 25.1%, 45.9%, 45%, 37.1%, and 19.5%, after 120 min, respectively. Also, the photo destruction of mentioned dyes under the same condition was studied for sample 7. As illustrated in Fig. 9e, the percentages were about 72.9%, 27.5%, 51.9%, 58.2%, 43.2%, and 21.9%. As can be seen in Fig. 9, the presence of GQDs and doping with CaSiO3 improve photocatalytic effectiveness. Also, the results depict that sample 7 with high-level doping of GQDs shows better photocatalytic performance than sample 6 with low-level doping of GQDs. Because GQDs increase the catalyst active surface, and also can constructively hinder the quick recombination of excitons with a large exciton binding energy and enhance the dye degradation [17,44]. Moreover, the rate constants for all reactions of organic dyes were calculated with Langmuir–Hinshelwood mechanism [45].
ln(C0/C) = kt

C0 is the primary dye concentration, C is the organic pollutant concentration at reaction time (t), and k is the Pseudo-first order rate coefficient (min−1) [46]. The first-order rate coefficient (k) was gained from linear relationships of ln(C0/C) against the reaction time. As illustrated in Fig. 9 (b, d, and f), samples with higher photocatalytic activity have a higher reaction rate coefficient. Fig. 10 a, c, and 10e show the photocatalytic ability of three different dosages of sample 7 (0.03, 0.05, and 0.07 g) in three different concentrations of Acid Red (10, 15, and 20 ppm), respectively. Although the degradation rate decreased with increasing dye concentration, enhancing the catalyst dosage in the dye solution increased the degradation percentage for all three concentrations of sample 7–72.9%, 41.7%, and 27.3% for 10, 15, and 20 ppm, severally. In Fig. 10 (b, d, and f), the Pseudo rate constant (k) was studied from Langmuir–Hinshelwood mechanism and shows the maximum photocatalytic activity was obtained with a higher reaction rate coefficient. Fig. 11 a indicates the impact of pH solution on the degradation of AR. The percentage of degradation for sample 7 was about 47.5%, 72.9%, and 78.5% in alkaline, neutral, and acidic media, respectively. The outcomes show that the highest performance is at an acidic medium (pH = 2.5). Since acid red is an anionic dye, it performs effectively at pH < 7 by increasing the concentration of H+ groups in the solution.

Fig. 11c indicates the influence of active species on photocatalytic performance by scavenging the active species with a group of quenchers for sample 7. The scavengers which used in this study were EDTA for h+, BA for •OH, and BQ for O2•− [47–49]. In the presence of BA and EDTA, the degradation of dyes showed a negligible decrease compared to no scavenger. When BQ was utilized as the •O2 − scavenger, a significant reduction manifested in the photocatalytic ability of sample 7. Overall, the effect of different scavengers proves that •O2 − has the main impact, and •OH or h+ represented an insignificant part in CaSiO3/GQDs nanocomposites activity.

It has been proven that when a suspension is exposed to light with higher light energy than its bandgap, electrons (e–) of the conduction band (CB) and holes (h+) of the valence band (VB) are generated [50]. Multiple reactive species such as H•, 1O2, •O2 −, and h+ are generated during the photocatalytic processes [51]. In this process, the •O2 − (radical of superoxide anion) is generated by dissolving O2 in water or adsorbed on the surface of catalyst and reducing it to •O2 −. Also, the •OH might be produced with several stage reduction of •O2 − in the e− → •O2 − → H2O2 → •OH way. These radicals, along with other high oxidant species (peroxide radicals), have been reported to be in charge of the photodestruction of toxic colorants. All corresponding photodegradation reactions on the surface of the catalyst for eliminating the toxic pollutants can be noted below [52–56]:
CaSiO3/GQDs nancomposites + hν → e− + h++ CaSiO3/GQDs nancomposites∗
e− + O2 → •O2 −
H2O + h+ → H+ + •OH
H+ + •O2 − + e− → HOO•
HOO• + H2O → H2O2 + •OH
2H+ + •O2 − → H2O2
H2O2 + e− → OH− + •OH
OH− + h+ → •OH
H2O + h+ → H+ + •OH
h+ + Organic dyes → •Organic dyes+→ Destruction products (e.g., H2O, CO2)
•O2 − or •OH + Organic dyes → Destruction products
•O2 − or •OH + •Organic dyes+ → Destruction products

The recyclability of CaSiO3/GQDs (sample 7) was studied during five periods of recycling under the same situations (Fig. 11d). After each test, the catalyst was washed (with water and ethanol), centrifuged, dried at 65 °C overnight, and re-employed for the next test. The result indicated the high stability of CaSiO3/GQDs nanocomposites and their acceptable photocatalytic ability during five reaction cycles. Results revealed that the photocatalytic activity was reduced by 1.5% after the fifth period.

In summary, the hydrothermal method was applied to prepare CaSiO3 and CaSiO3/GQDs nanocomposites. The influence of various surface-active agents was studied to obtain the best structure and purity of CaSiO3 nanostructures. Also, GQDs solution was prepared via hydrothermal route using cotton as a natural precursor. The photocatalytic treatment of CaSiO3 and CaSiO3/GQDs nanocomposites was studied as a valuable and low-cost technology for eliminating organic pollutants from water. DRS information confirmed that the bandgap of CaSiO3 decreased from 3.4 to 2.55 eV by doping GQDs in its pure phase and showed that it could be suitable for photocatalytic application. The results revealed that the incorporation of GQDs could improve the photocatalytic performance of CaSiO3 for degrading five different dyes and especially AR in water. More importantly, in a high level of doping CaSiO3 with GQDs solution, the photocatalytic ability of the nanocomposite developed under visible light. Besides, the kinetics of photocatalytic reactions revealed that higher photocatalytic efficiency possessed a higher reaction rate constant. We nominate CaSiO3/GQDs nanocomposites as high efficient photocatalysts for water treatment in the visible region.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are appreciative of the committee of Iran National Science Foundation; INSF (97017837), and the University of Kashan for financing this research by Grant No (159271/MK5).
